name: Oro
description: Oro stands as a prominent deity in Polynesian mythology, particularly revered in the Society Islands, including Tahiti. He is often associated with war, fertility, and creation, holding a position of significant power and influence within the pantheon. Oro's worship was often characterized by elaborate rituals and ceremonies, reflecting his elevated status. Notably, the 'Oro cult' played a vital role in shaping political and social structures, with chiefs and warriors seeking his favor to gain power and success in battle. He is frequently depicted with feathered garments, a testament to his connection with the divine realm and the natural world. The god's impact on Polynesian culture is undeniable, leaving a lasting legacy in art, religion, and societal norms. Through ritual and legend, Oro embodies a complex tapestry of power, creation, and the cyclical nature of life and death, solidifying his place as a central figure in the beliefs of the Polynesian people. His worship was eventually suppressed by the introduction of Christianity, but elements of his mythology persist in cultural memory and artistic expression.
subtitle: God of War and Fertility

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World by Oro
keyMyths.description: In one version of the creation myth, Oro plays a pivotal role in bringing the world into existence. Emerging from the void, he, along with other deities, shapes the land, the sea, and the sky. Oro's specific contribution often involves introducing fertility and abundance to the newly formed world, ensuring the continuation of life. This myth highlights Oro's creative power and his connection to the natural world, solidifying his position as a vital force in the Polynesian cosmology. The story underscores the importance of Oro in maintaining the balance of the universe.
keyMyths.source: Henry, Teuira. *Ancient Tahiti*. Bernice P. Bishop Museum Bulletin 48. Honolulu: Bishop Museum Press, 1928.

keyMyths.title: Oro and the Maro 'Ura
keyMyths.description: The Maro 'Ura, a sacred red feather girdle, is closely associated with Oro. Legend states that it was a gift from the gods, representing divine power and authority. Possession of the Maro 'Ura was a symbol of chiefly status and conferred legitimacy upon rulers. The girdle was also believed to possess protective qualities, ensuring success in warfare and prosperity for the land. The myth of Oro and the Maro 'Ura highlights the god's influence on the political and social order of Polynesian society, establishing a clear link between divine favor and earthly power.
keyMyths.source: Orsmond, John Muggridge. *The History of the Tahitian Mission, 1799-1830*. Edited by B. Henry. London: The London Missionary Society, 1933.

keyMyths.title: Oro's Relationship with Humans
keyMyths.description: Myths often depict Oro interacting with humans, sometimes through intermediaries like priests or chiefs. These interactions typically involve offerings, prayers, and rituals designed to appease the god and secure his blessings. Human sacrifices were sometimes performed in Oro's honor, reflecting the perceived need to offer something of great value to the deity. These stories reveal the complex relationship between humans and the divine in Polynesian culture, emphasizing the importance of maintaining a harmonious balance between the two realms. They also demonstrate the significant role of religious practice in shaping daily life.
keyMyths.source: Ellis, William. *Polynesian Researches, During a Residence of Nearly Eight Years in the Society and Sandwich Islands*. London: Fisher, Son, & Jackson, 1831.

sources.author: Henry, Teuira
